final_prompt.prompt:
Create an original, non-infringing medical education illustration of the human cardiovascular system as a 3D anatomical visualization, for healthcare science communication.

Style/medium: photorealistic medical illustration in the tradition of Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy, combined with modern 3D rendering — precise anatomical draftsmanship, volumetric depth, subsurface scattering on tissue surfaces
Composition: centered frontal view of the heart with a cross-sectional cut revealing internal chambers, major vessels extending outward, generous surrounding negative space reserved for annotation labels
Subject: human heart sectioned open to display all four chambers — left atrium, right atrium, left ventricle, right ventricle — with valve positions and morphology clearly visible (mitral, tricuspid, aortic, pulmonary valves); aorta, pulmonary artery, superior vena cava, inferior vena cava rendered in full; coronary arteries traced along the epicardial surface highlighted in gold; myocardial fiber texture visible on cut surfaces; directional blood-flow arrows with dynamic light-trail effects indicating circulation path
Scene/backdrop: deep dark background (near-black, slightly cool-toned) to maximize anatomical contrast and clarity
Lighting/mood: focused rim lighting and internal glow to separate structures; soft specular highlights on vessel walls; clinical and precise, not dramatic
Color palette: arterial system in vivid oxygenated red; venous system in deep navy blue; coronary artery highlights in warm gold; myocardium in natural deep-rose tissue tone; background near-black — each system color-coded for immediate educational legibility
Materials/textures: smooth glistening endocardial lining, striated myocardial fiber bundles on cut face, semi-translucent vessel walls with visible wall thickness, matte epicardial surface
Constraints: label annotation space must be preserved around all major structures; image must remain legible at textbook print size; color-coding must be consistent and unambiguous for medical education use
Avoid: cartoon or stylized anatomy, excessive lens flare, decorative non-anatomical elements, inaccurate chamber proportions, merged or indistinct valve structures
